title: Pauper's Ladder - a perfect level of abundance (a comprehensive overview)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=i6kooD4epJ0

Daniel (@dungeondive) delivers a comprehensive, everything-in-one-place overview of Pauper's Ladder (Bedsit Games, UK-only distribution), arguing it has reached a "perfect state of abundance" — big and epic, yet contained and complete, like Iron Helm or Quest for the Lost Pixel. He walks the whole ecosystem: the competitive base game (race to three of five virtues across an open Talisman/Runebound-style crawl, bird companions, recipe-unlocked skills, card-draw combat); the limited "One Lonely Popper" scenario solo mode (co-designed with Russ Law of Bag of Dungeon); and the Bodok mini-expansion of four supernatural paupers with familiars.

He covers Moon Towers in full — its talents/curses, 14 birds, robust four-difficulty solo mode with hit points, and the beloved Illustrated Field Guide achievement-coloring book — then the latest Cobbled Isle expansion: a whole city-encounter region (the Dungeon Dive itself is a visitable location), a roaming merchant ship (the Cy Ren) with a little economy, a dice-den gambling minigame, per-pauper personal quests, a short "Timely Popper" two-virtue mode, and a genuine co-op "We the Collective" threat-management mode.

His two wishes: a single comprehensive rulebook and standees for the paupers. Otherwise a bold, charming, one-of-a-kind indie he'd rebuy instantly if lost.

----

Does a game reaching this "perfect abundance" — big but complete, fully modular — appeal more than an endless expansion treadmill?
